summary: Martin uses the Volkswagen emissions scandal as a cautionary tale for software engineers about the limits of 'just following orders.' He describes the likely scene where engineers were asked to defeat emissions tests and probably found the technical challenge intellectually appealing. The post notes that engineer James Liang received 40 months in prison and $200,000 in fines despite arguing he was following orders out of loyalty to his employer. Martin concludes that engineers will be held to high ethical standards by courts regardless of employer pressure.
key_points: The VW emissions defeat device was likely seen as a 'cool problem' by the engineers who built it
The 'just following orders' defense failed - James Liang received near-maximum sentencing
Courts imposed 10x the requested fine specifically as a deterrent to other engineers
Your employer cannot protect you from legal consequences of unethical work
Engineers are held to high ethical standards whether their employers uphold those standards or not
money_quotes: What a cool problem to have to solve? No, really! Imagine how much fun it would be to figure out some sneaky way to bypass the emission test.
Their defense, of course, was that they were just following orders. They were just protecting their jobs – making sure they could feed their families. But that defense didn't fly.
Engineers take note: Your employer can't cover for you.
Doing your job does not mean that you just follow orders.
The courts are going to hold you to a high ethical standard, even if your employer does not.
key_insight: Engineers bear personal legal and ethical responsibility for the code they write, and 'just following orders' provides no protection when that code causes harm.
